definition: A concise summary of what the tool does and its primary purpose
formatOptions: 1-3 sentences describing core functionality and value proposition

definition: The primary individual(s), organization, or company responsible for maintaining and developing the tool
formatOptions: Person name(s) or organization name

definition: Geographic location where the primary development team or organization is based
formatOptions: Country name or region (e.g., "USA", "Europe", "Germany")

definition: The year when the project was first initiated or announced
formatOptions: Month and year

definition: Current state of development and availability
formatOptions: Released, Alpha, Beta, WIP (Work In Progress), Defunct, Planned

definition: The license and availability of the project's source code
formatOptions: Permissive (Apache, MIT, BSD), Libre (GPL, AGPL), Closed source
notes: Include link to repository when applicable

definition: The original use case or domain from which the tool emerged
formatOptions: Chat, File sharing, Productivity, Database, Ecosystem/SDK, Knowledge Base, Social Network

definition: Primary programming languages and runtime environments used
formatOptions: Rust, JavaScript/TypeScript, Python, Golang, JVM/Clojure, C/C++, Erlang, PHP, Haskell
notes: May include multiple languages separated by "+"

definition: The underlying data model and storage architecture
formatOptions: CRDTs (Conflict-free replicated data types), ACID monolithic, Distributed (consensus-based), DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph), or specific database name

definition: The language or API used to query and manipulate data
formatOptions: Examples: SPARQL, GraphQL, Datalog, Custom, SQL, LDP (Linked Data Platform)
notes: May support multiple query languages

definition: The file formats and data structures supported for import, export, and storage
formatOptions: JSON, JSON-LD, RDF, Markdown, XML, Custom, ActivityPub, Org-mode
notes: Multiple formats separated by "+"

definition: Support for real-time simultaneous editing by multiple users
formatOptions: Collaborative real-time editing, OT based live editing (Operational Transformation), N/A, Planned
notes: Indicates the technology used (CRDTs, OT, etc.)

definition: Support for formatted text with styling, images, and rich media
formatOptions: Rich Text editing, Basic text only, N/A
notes: Includes capabilities like bold, italic, embedded images, etc.

definition: Availability of native or web-based mobile applications
formatOptions: Mobile support, Partial Mobile support, React Native, N/A
notes: Distinguish between native apps and responsive web apps

definition: Ability to run in web browsers
formatOptions: Yes or No
notes: May run as progressive web app (PWA) or traditional web application

definition: Availability of platform-specific desktop applications
formatOptions: Native apps, Electron Desktop app, Flutter, N/A
notes: Includes Windows, macOS, Linux applications

definition: Business model and access terms for using the tool
formatOptions: Self-hosting, Edge device, Freemium SaaS, Licensed install, Free SaaS, Paid SaaS
notes: Multiple terms may apply (e.g., "Self-hosting + Freemium SaaS")

definition: Primary source of financial support
formatOptions: VC (Venture Capital), NGI (Next Generation Internet), ICO (Initial Coin Offering), N/A, Personal investment, Undisclosed

definition: Amount of funding raised
formatOptions: Dollar/Euro/etc. amount with source (e.g., "$9M", "€24M")
notes: Include link to announcement when available

definition: The underlying protocol(s) or technology stack this protocol builds upon
formatOptions: Examples may include IPFS, Holochain, NextGraph, Matrix, datahike, specific blockchain implementations, etc.
notes: Empty if original/foundational protocol

definition: The peer-to-peer networking architecture and consensus mechanism used
formatOptions: Byzantine causal broadcast, DHT (Distributed Hash Table), Federation, Mesh network, Public/Private Blockchain, Simple pub/sub
notes: For Protocols: Describes how nodes discover and communicate with each other. For Platforms: May differ from underlying protocol if platform adds abstraction layer.

definition: The logical network topology created on top of the physical network
formatOptions: Global, App-wide, Store-wide, App-wide two-tier, Store-wide two-tier, N/A
notes: "Global" means data scattered across entire network; "Store-wide" means limited to members of a specific data store

definition: Whether content is addressed by cryptographic hash rather than location
formatOptions: Yes or No
notes: Enables verifiable, location-independent data retrieval (e.g., IPFS CIDs)

definition: Whether the primary data source resides on user devices with cloud as backup
formatOptions: Local-First, Local-only, Offline first, N/A
notes: "Local-First" means full offline capability with sync; "Local-only" means no sync capability

definition: End-to-end encryption support ensuring only sender and recipient can read data
formatOptions: Yes or No
notes: Data encrypted on sender device, decrypted only on recipient device

definition: Specific Conflict-free Replicated Data Type libraries implemented or supported
formatOptions: Automerge, Yrs/Yjs, Custom CRDTs, Differential Dataflow, RDF SU-set
notes: CRDTs enable automatic conflict resolution in distributed systems

definition: Ability to reach consensus even when some nodes are malicious or faulty
formatOptions: Yes or No
notes: Critical for trustless distributed systems

definition: Cryptographic signature mechanism used for authentication and integrity
formatOptions: BLS Threshold signature, Ed25519, Signature, N/A
notes: Ensures data authenticity and non-repudiation

definition: Access control model for managing data and resource permissions
formatOptions: Cryptographic Capabilities, ACL (Access Control Lists), UCANs (User Controlled Authorization Networks), OCAP (Object Capabilities), OAuth/OpenID, CCAP (Convergent Capabilities)
notes: May combine multiple models (e.g., "Cryptographic Capabilities + ACL")

definition: Compatibility with RDF, JSON-LD, SPARQL. Interoperability with semantic web ecosystem.
formatOptions: Full RDF (Resource Description Framework) compliance, Links, SOLID, Custom, N/A
notes: Enables machine-readable linked data and interoperability

definition: Support for programmable, self-executing contracts on the protocol
formatOptions: Yes, No, Planned for future
notes: Enables trustless automation of business logic

definition: Whether the protocol operates on top of existing TCP/IP infrastructure (the vast majority of P2P protocols) or operates at the transport/packet level itself as a parallel or replacement layer.
formatOptions: Values: Application-layer (built on TCP/IP) / Transport-equivalent (packet-level) / Cross-layer.

definition: Whether the protocol natively embeds asset ownership and value transfer at the packet or message level, or whether value is handled externally.
formatOptions: Values: Native (packet-level) / External ledger / Application-layer add-on / None.

definition: SDKs, APIs, and tooling provided for application developers
formatOptions: List of available tools and languages supported
notes: REST API, GraphQL API, JavaScript SDK, Mobile SDK, CLI tools

definition: How applications built on this platform can be deployed
formatOptions: Self-hosted servers, Edge devices, Cloud hosting, Hybrid deployment
notes: Describes infrastructure options for app deployment

definition: Identity management system provided by the platform
formatOptions: Decentralized ID (DID), Self-Sovereign Identity (SSI), OAuth/OpenID, Custom, Blockchain-based, Federated Identity, Delegated Identity, Hybrid, Custodial, Non-custodial
notes: How users are identified and authenticated across applications

definition: How the platform handles data storage and replication
formatOptions: Distributed storage, Local storage with sync, Hybrid cloud-edge storage, User wallet (local), Encrypted cloud storage, Distributed storage (IPFS, Arweave), Blockchain/Ledger, Hybrid (local + backup), No storage (ephemeral), Browser storage, Mobile secure enclave.
notes: Describes where and how application data is persisted

definition: Ability to work with other platforms, protocols, or standards
formatOptions: List of supported protocols, data formats, or federation capabilities. Examples: ActivityPub support, IPFS integration, Cross-chain bridges

definition: Whether social graph backend feature is supported
formatOptions: Yes or No

definition: Primary application domain or user need addressed
formatOptions: Knowledge Management, Communication/Chat, File Sharing, Productivity, Social Network, Collaboration, Media/Publishing, Note-taking
notes: An app may serve multiple use cases

definition: The style and paradigm of the user interface
formatOptions: Document-centric, Graph-based, Spreadsheet, Timeline/Feed, Canvas, Chat interface, File browser
notes: Describes how users interact with the application

definition: What functionality is available without network connectivity
formatOptions: Full functionality offline, Read-only offline, Sync required, Online only
notes: Critical for local-first applications

definition: Specific features enabling multi-user collaboration
formatOptions: List of available features. Examples: Real-time editing, Comments/annotations, Presence indicators, Version history, Conflict resolution

definition: Ability to export and migrate data to other tools
formatOptions: Full export (all formats), Partial export, Proprietary format only, Standards-based export

definition: Third-party integrations and extensions available
formatOptions: Browser extensions, Mobile apps, API integrations, Plugin marketplace
notes: Indicates extensibility and ecosystem maturity

definition: Ability to import data from traditional centralized services
formatOptions: Examples: Import from Google Docs, Notion, Evernote, Twitter

definition: Whether features that support collective intelligence and healthy community dynamics are included
formatOptions: Options might include Moderation tools, Reputation systems, Governance mechanisms

definition: Whether features that provide economic flows, possibly in the form of tokenomics, micropayments, resource deployment, and/or financial irrigation methods are included
formatOptions: Options might include Cryptocurrency support, Fiat payments, Smart contracts for payments

definition: Whether governance and decision making features are included
formatOptions: Options might include Core team, DAO, Community voting, Foundation, Foundation-governed, Company-controlled, Consortium, W3C/IETF standards body, Multi-stakeholder

definition: Whether media and publishing features are included
formatOptions: Yes or No

definition: Whether knowledge management features are included
formatOptions: Yes or No

definition: Whether features that help users synthesize information and derive insights are included
formatOptions: Yes or No

definition: Whether project management features are included
formatOptions: Yes or No

definition: The primary interaction pattern the application supports, describing whether users must be online simultaneously for the core use case to function.
formatOptions: Synchronous (real-time chat, video, live co-editing) / Asynchronous (email-like, forum, store-and-forward) / Collaborative (simultaneous multi-user document or workspace editing) / Transactional (exchanges, transfers, one-off interactions) / Hybrid (combines multiple models)
notes: An app may support multiple models; list primary first. "Collaborative" overlaps with "Synchronous" but is distinguished by shared-artifact focus rather than conversational focus. Relates to but is distinct from "Collaborative Live Editing" which captures capability rather than interaction pattern.

definition: How the application handles degraded, intermittent, or absent network connectivity, including offline operation and sync-on-reconnect behavior.
formatOptions: Full offline (works without any connectivity, syncs on reconnect) / Delay-tolerant (store-and-forward, mesh relay, gossip-based delivery) / Partial offline (read/draft offline, requires connection to send/commit) / Online-required (core functionality requires active connection) / Mesh-capable (can form ad-hoc local networks via BLE, Wi-Fi Direct, LoRa, etc.) Particularly relevant for serverless/P2P apps where no central server guarantees message delivery. Closely related to "Local-First" attribute but focuses on operational behavior rather than architectural principle. Multiple values may apply (e.g., "Full offline + Mesh-capable").

definition: How users find and connect with other users or join shared spaces without relying on a central directory or server.
formatOptions: DHT (distributed hash table lookup) / QR/Out-of-band (physical or side-channel key/address exchange) / Invite link (shareable URIs encoding connection info) / DNS-like (name resolution system, e.g., NDN naming, .eth domains) / Social graph (friend-of-friend, web of trust traversal) / Relay/Rendezvous (lightweight server assists connection but doesn't mediate communication) / Broadcast (multicast, mDNS, local network announcement)
notes: Many apps combine mechanisms (e.g., relay for initial discovery + DHT for ongoing). Distinct from "Authentication & Identity" which covers verification of who someone is, while Discovery covers how you find them in the first place.

definition: The practical upper bound on simultaneous participants in a single session, conversation, or shared workspace, as constrained by protocol, CRDT performance, or network topology.
formatOptions: 1:1 (dyadic only) / Small group (2–10) / Medium group (10–100) / Large group (100–1,000) / Broadcast (1,000+) / Unbounded (no architectural limit) / Unknown
notes: Record the practical tested or documented limit, not theoretical maximum. Where performance degrades gracefully, note the threshold (e.g., "Medium group; CRDT sync degrades above ~50 concurrent editors"). Protocol-level limits may differ from UX-level limits.

definition: How servers federate and share data. Distinguishes federation architectures.
formatOptions: Server-to-Server (S2S), Shared Heap (ATProto-style), Hub-and-Spoke, Full mesh

definition: What's needed to run a participating server. Affects decentralization potential.
formatOptions: Technical requirements, complexity level
notes: ATProto Relay is "a fairly resource-demanding service" vs ActivityPub instances can be modest

definition: Can users move between servers keeping their identity/data. Key differentiator between protocols.
formatOptions: Full portability, Partial portability, Server-locked, DID-based

definition: How content is discovered across federated network. Affects discoverability and centralization risks.
formatOptions: Instance-based, Global relay/firehose, Hybrid, Search indexers

definition: What control servers have over their users. Governance implications.
formatOptions: Full authority, Shared authority, Minimal (data-only), User-controlled

definition: Standardization status. Affects long-term stability.
formatOptions: W3C Standard, W3C Draft / Recommendation Track, IETF Standard (RFC), IETF Draft, IEEE Standard, ISO Standard, Proprietary Open Standard, Community Standard, De Facto Standard, Academic / Research, Pre-specification
notes: • IETF Standard (RFC) — A finalized, published RFC. The current list has "IETF Draft" but no option for completed IETF standards. Many P2P protocols build on or become RFCs. • W3C Draft / Recommendation Track — W3C has a multi-stage process (Working Draft → Candidate Recommendation → Proposed Recommendation → Recommendation). "W3C Standard" only captures the endpoint. Some identity and data protocols are actively on this track but not yet Recommendations. • IEEE Standard — Relevant for some P2P and networking protocols (e.g., mesh networking standards). • ISO Standard — Relevant for identity protocols especially (ISO 18013-5 for mDL, ISO 27001 references). Also relevant for some data protocols. • Academic / Research — Protocols emerging from university research with published papers but no formal standards body involvement (e.g., NDN from UCLA, some CRDT protocols). Common in P2P Pro. • De Facto Standard — Widely adopted without formal standardization body endorsement. Protocol achieves standard status through market adoption rather than committee process (e.g., Nostr, some blockchain protocols). • Pre-specification — Design or concept stage; no formal specification document yet published. Relevant for ID Sys-adjacent protocols and early-stage work.

definition: Where user data is stored and who controls it. Privacy and ownership implications.
formatOptions: Server-controlled, User-controlled via PDS, Distributed, Hybrid

definition: What domain the vocabulary describes. Defines the protocol's scope.
formatOptions: Economic flows, Social relationships, General-purpose

definition: How the protocol is typically used. Clarifies relationship to other protocols.
formatOptions: Embedded in other protocols, Standalone, API overlay

definition: Which decentralized identity specifications and standards are supported or implemented
formatOptions: W3C DID (Decentralized Identifiers), W3C Verifiable Credentials, DIDComm, OpenID Connect for Verifiable Credentials (OIDC4VC), FIDO2/WebAuthn, BBS+ Signatures, Self-Issued OpenID Provider v2 (SIOPv2)
notes: Examples: W3C DID: Decentralized identifier specification, W3C VC: Verifiable credentials data model, DIDComm: Secure messaging protocol for DIDs

definition: Which specific DID method(s) the system supports for creating and resolving decentralized identifiers
formatOptions: did:key, did:web, did:ion, did:ethr, did:pkh, did:peer, did:polygon, did:sov, did:plc, Multiple/Custom

definition: How cryptographic keys are generated, stored, and managed
formatOptions: User-controlled (local wallet), Hardware security module (HSM), Multi-party computation (MPC), Cloud key management, Browser/OS keychain, Smart contract, Biometric-protected, Social recovery, Threshold signatures. Format: Primary method + recovery options (e.g., "Hardware wallet + Social recovery")

definition: What types of verifiable credentials or claims the system supports
formatOptions: Verifiable Credentials (VCs), Self-Attested Claims, Third-Party Attestations, Educational Credentials, Professional Certifications, Government IDs, Membership Proofs, Reputation Scores, Token-Gated Access, ZK-Proofs

definition: How credentials and identity claims are verified
formatOptions: Cryptographic signature verification, Zero-knowledge proofs (ZKPs), Blockchain anchoring, Trusted registries, Biometric verification, Multi-factor authentication, Selective disclosure, Credential revocation lists

definition: Privacy-enhancing technologies and capabilities built into the system
formatOptions: Zero-knowledge proofs, Selective disclosure, Unlinkability (pairwise DIDs), Blinded credentials, Minimal disclosure, Credential anonymization, Anti-correlation, Privacy-preserving revocation

definition: How users prove control of their identity when authenticating
formatOptions: Cryptographic signatures, Biometrics, Passkeys/WebAuthn, QR code scanning, Deep linking, Passwordless, Hardware tokens, Multi-factor (MFA), Challenge-response

definition: How credentials can be invalidated or revoked
formatOptions: Revocation lists (on-chain/off-chain), Status lists, Accumulator-based, Time-bound expiration, Self-revocation, Issuer revocation, Privacy-preserving revocation (StatusList2021)

definition: What types of entities can have identities in the system
formatOptions: Humans (individuals), Organizations, DAOs/Collectives, Autonomous agents/AI, Devices/IoT, Services/APIs, Pseudonymous entities

definition: What software can be used to manage the identity
formatOptions: Mobile app, Browser extension, Desktop application, Hardware wallet, Web-based (custodial), Command-line tool, SDK/Library integration

definition: How users can recover access if they lose keys or credentials
formatOptions: Social recovery (guardians), Seed phrase backup, Cloud backup (encrypted), Hardware backup, Time-locked recovery, Multi-signature recovery, No recovery (user responsible), Account abstraction

definition: Which regulatory frameworks or compliance standards the system addresses
formatOptions: GDPR compliant, eIDAS (EU regulation), KYC/AML support, CCPA, HIPAA, SOC 2, ISO 27001, NIST guidelines, Age verification (without revealing age)

definition: Protocols used for issuing, presenting, and verifying credentials
formatOptions: DIDComm, OIDC4VC (OpenID for Verifiable Credentials), CHAPI (Credential Handler API), Presentation Exchange, Verifiable Presentation Request

definition: What establishes trust in the identity system
formatOptions: Web of Trust, Trusted issuer registries, Blockchain consensus, Cryptographic verification only, Government-anchored, Reputation systems

definition: Economic model for using the identity system
formatOptions: Free (no blockchain fees), Gas fees (blockchain transactions), Subscription, Per-credential fee, Freemium, Enterprise licensing

definition: Ability to maintain identity even if services are shut down or accounts banned
formatOptions: Fully censorship-resistant (blockchain/P2P), Depends on DID method, Service provider dependent, Hybrid

definition: The primary service this infrastructure provides to the network or to higher-layer applications and users.
formatOptions: Censorship circumvention, Anonymous routing, Relay / proxy, Distributed storage, Name resolution, Bandwidth sharing, Mesh transport, VPN tunneling, Mixnet, Bridge / gateway

definition: The adversarial conditions this infrastructure is designed to withstand or the security/privacy guarantees it provides to users.
formatOptions: State-level censorship, Mass surveillance, Traffic analysis, Network shutdown, DPI (Deep Packet Inspection), ISP-level blocking, Targeted surveillance, Metadata exposure

definition: How the infrastructure's operational nodes are provisioned and who runs them.
formatOptions: Volunteer-operated, Foundation-operated, Commercially hosted, Self-hosted, Hybrid (volunteer + commercial), Incentivized (token/payment), Embedded (runs on user devices automatically)

definition: What the infrastructure operator or relay node can observe about the traffic passing through it.
formatOptions: No content (encrypted payload only), No metadata (source/destination hidden), No content + no metadata, Content visible, Metadata visible, Partial metadata (e.g. destination but not source)

definition: The current operational scale of the infrastructure in terms of nodes, users, or traffic volume.
formatOptions: Free text (e.g., "~7,000 relay nodes, ~2M daily users" or "200,000+ Conduit stations activated Jan 2026" or "Pre-deployment")

definition: Whether and how data stored or published through this protocol/network can be modified after initial creation.
formatOptions: Immutable / Append-only / Mutable (versioned) / Mutable (latest only) / Mutable via CRDTs / Smart-contract governed / Configurable (operator/user choice)
notes: Immutable and append-only are stronger guarantees against censorship and data manipulation. Mutable systems often use content addressing for individual versions while providing a mutable pointer (e.g. IPNS). Where multiple policies coexist (e.g. immutable content CIDs with mutable IPNS pointers), note both.

definition: The primary use-case domain the standard is designed to serve.
formatOptions: Agent identity / Agent reputation & trust / Agent discovery / Agent payments / Data integrity / Access control / Asset ownership / Cross-chain interoperability / General purpose (tag all that apply)
notes: ERC-8004 spans agent identity, agent reputation & trust, and agent discovery simultaneously.

definition: How the standard is instantiated in practice — is it a per-chain singleton (one canonical deployment per blockchain network), permissionlessly deployable by anyone (many instances may exist), factory-deployed (a root contract spawns child instances), or off-chain verifiable (the standard defines cryptographic formats that don't require on-chain deployment at all, such as DID methods or VC schemas)?
formatOptions: Per-chain singleton / Permissionless multi-instance / Factory pattern / Off-chain verifiable / Hybrid (on-chain registry + off-chain resolution)

definition: How node operators cryptographically prove they are actually storing the data they claim to store. This is the technical heart of what distinguishes decentralized storage from simple replication.
formatOptions: Values: Proof of Replication (PoRep) / Proof of Spacetime (PoSt) / Proof of Access (PoA) / Erasure coding with audit / Merkle audit proof / None / Hybrid.

definition: How node operators are compensated for providing storage. This is structurally different from anything in P2P Pro because storage networks require explicit economic incentives to function — unlike relay nodes which are often purely volunteer.
formatOptions: Values: Token rewards (native token) / Fiat/stablecoin payments / One-time endowment (permanent storage model) / Volunteer / Hybrid.

definition: Whether the network provides any formal guarantee that stored data can be retrieved, and under what conditions.
formatOptions: Values: Strong (cryptoeconomic enforcement) / Best-effort (incentivized but not guaranteed) / Content-addressed only (available if any node caches it) / Owner-controlled / None.

definition: The structural role or architectural layer the standard occupies within the broader smart contract ecosystem. Classifies the standard by what kind of interface or behavior it specifies, independent of its application domain.
formatOptions: Token Standard, Identity Standard, Account Standard, Interface Standard, Composability Standard, Agent/Coordination Standard, Cross-chain Standard. Values may be combined where a standard spans multiple roles (e.g. "Token Standard / Interface Standard").
notes: Focuses on architectural role, not application domain — e.g. ERC-165 and ERC-721 may share a domain but are structurally distinct standard types. List is intentionally open-ended to accommodate non-EVM standards.

definition: The blockchain platform, protocol family, or runtime environment for which the standard is natively designed. Identifies the chain or chain family within which the standard has authoritative status and primary adoption.
formatOptions: Ethereum, Cosmos, Polkadot, Solana, NEAR, Tezos, Cardano, Algorand, Bitcoin, Hyperledger, Cross-chain, Chain-agnostic
notes: Distinct from Stack (which captures implementation language) and Based On (which captures derivation). A standard may be deployable on multiple chains but should be recorded under its native ecosystem. Use "Cross-chain" for standards whose primary purpose is interoperability across ecosystems; use "Chain-agnostic" for standards that are not tied to any specific runtime.

definition: The the list of source documents and reference notes that support the information given in the other fields.
formatOptions: List of citations or notes in the format Scource: https://website | Note: giving explanation | ...
